methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $10M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 9 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: Hadrian
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C), post-money $1.6B

private.name: Nimble Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B

data.narrative:
Bear Robotics, a Series C-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by John Ha, has raised $175.8M to date. The valuation analysis draws on two methods triangulated against a peer universe of nine same-sector, same/adjacent-stage companies—eight private and one public—including 1X Technologies, Exotec, Bedrock Robotics, Dexterity, and Hadrian.

The Stage-Progression method applies a 1.7x multiplier typical of Series C advancement to a $10M total-raised proxy, yielding $17M. The Peer-Median approach anchors to a nine-company robotics cohort with a median valuation of $1.6B, a range spanning $300M to $10.0B. This method reflects current market values of comparable-stage peers: Hadrian at $1.6B (Series C), Dexterity at $1.6B (Series B), and Exotec at $2.0B (Series D).

The two methods produce a wide triangulated range: $14.5M (low) to $1.84B (high), with a median of $808M. The material spread reflects structural tension between backward-looking stage progression and forward-looking peer benchmarking in a high-growth, volatile sector. Key uncertainties include stage and trajectory comparability within the peer set, unit economics relative to named competitors, and sector-wide valuation compression or expansion. The peer-median method appears more informative given the established public and late-stage private comps in robotics; the stage-progression floor should be treated as a sanity check only.
